title: XML Intro
content:
XML Introduction

Data Transport

XML stands for eXtensible Markup Language. It is designed to store and transport data, and to be both human- and machine-readable. Unlike HTML, XML focuses on describing data rather than presenting it.

XML is widely used for data exchange between systems and applications.

title: Syntax Rules
content:
XML Syntax Rules

Writing XML

XML documents must follow simple syntax rules.

  • Every XML document must have exactly one root element.
  • Elements are case-sensitive and must be properly nested and closed.
  • Special characters must be escaped using entity references.

Well-formed XML adheres strictly to these syntax rules.

title: Tree Structure
content:
XML Tree Structure

The Hierarchy

An XML document forms a hierarchical tree structure starting at the root element and branching out to child elements.



Each node in the tree can have children, text content, and attributes. This tree structure allows programs to navigate and process XML data easily.

title: Elements
content:
XML Elements

Building Blocks

XML elements are the basic building blocks of an XML document. Elements consist of a start tag, content, and an end tag.

You can nest elements to represent complex structured data. Text between element tags is the data content of the element.

title: Attributes
content:
XML Attributes

Element Metadata

XML attributes provide additional information about elements. Attribute values must always be quoted.

Attributes are useful for metadata, but they cannot contain hierarchical or complex structures like nested elements.

title: Namespaces
content:
XML Namespaces

Avoiding Name Conflicts

XML namespaces are used to avoid name conflicts in XML documents by qualifying names of elements and attributes. A namespace is declared using the xmlns attribute with a prefix and a URI.



When combining XML vocabularies from multiple different sources, namespaces allow different elements with the exact same name to be distinguished and processed correctly without crashing the parser.

title: HttpRequest (AJAX)
content:
XML HttpRequest (AJAX)

Fetching Data

The XMLHttpRequest object can be used to request data from a web server without reloading the page. It enables AJAX functionality in web applications, allowing XML files to be loaded and processed dynamically using JavaScript.

Fetching XML
var xhttp = new XMLHttpRequest();
xhttp.onreadystatechange = function() {
  if (this.readyState == 4 && this.status == 200) {
    // This contains the parsed XML document
    console.log(this.responseXML);
  }
};
xhttp.open("GET", "data.xml", true);
xhttp.send();

This technique is widely used to fetch XML data and update HTML content interactively.

title: XML DOM
content:
XML DOM

Document Object Model

The XML DOM (Document Object Model) defines a standard way of representing and interacting with XML documents as a tree structure.



With the XML DOM, you can access, modify, add, or delete elements using standard methods in many programming languages. This is essential for applications that need to manipulate XML data programmatically.

title: DTD
content:
XML DTD

Document Type Definition

Document Type Definition (DTD) defines the legal structure and allowed elements or attributes of an XML document. A valid XML document must be well-formed and conform strictly to its DTD.

Using a DTD helps enforce consistency and rules for XML data structures across massive enterprise systems.

title: Schema (XSD)
content:
XML Schema (XSD)

Advanced Validation

XML Schema (XSD) describes the structure and data types allowed in an XML document, providing far more control than a DTD.

XML Schema uses actual XML syntax and supports defining elements, attributes, and strict data types such as strings, numbers, and dates. Using an XML Schema makes XML validation stricter and more precise.

title: Web Services
content:
XML Web Services

Network Communication

XML is widely used in web services. Web Service Description Language (WSDL), Simple Object Access Protocol (SOAP), RDF (Resource Description Framework), and RSS are XML-based standards for describing, accessing, and exchanging information over networks.



These XML technologies are foundational to interoperable web services, allowing servers written in entirely different programming languages to communicate flawlessly.

title: XPath Intro
content:
XML XPath Introduction

Navigating the Tree

XPath (XML Path Language) is used to navigate and select nodes (elements, attributes, text, etc.) in an XML document using path-like expressions.



XPath functions and expressions allow you to find specific parts of an XML tree by following steps similar to file system paths. It is widely used in XSLT and scripting environments to query XML.

title: XPath Examples
content:
Basic XPath Examples

Finding Elements

XPath expressions can select elements and values from XML by following specific paths.

Path Syntax
-- Selects all book titles from the bookstore node
/bookstore/book/title

-- Selects ONLY the first book’s title
/bookstore/book[1]/title

XPath can also select nodes based on specific conditions and instantly extract text or values from those nodes.

title: Nodes & Operators
content:
XPath Nodes and Operators

Traversing Relationships

In XPath, nodes represent elements, attributes, and other parts of an XML document structure. XPath includes operators such as =, !=, >, and boolean operators like and and or to build complex expressions.

XPath also provides axes to traverse relationships such as child, parent, descendant, and sibling nodes.

title: XSLT Intro
content:
XML XSLT Introduction

Transforming Data

XSLT (eXtensible Stylesheet Language Transformations) is used to transform XML documents into other formats such as HTML or another XML structure. It is more powerful than simple stylesheet languages (like CSS) because it can add, remove, and rearrange elements and attributes in the output.



XSLT stylesheets use XPath to specify exactly which parts of the input XML document to transform.

title: XSLT Templates
content:
XSLT Templates

Matching Nodes

XSLT templates define transformation rules that apply when specific XML nodes are matched. The <xsl:template> element contains instructions that determine how the matched XML content should be processed and output.

Template Match
<xsl:template match="/">
  <html>
    <body>
      <h2>My Book Collection</h2>
    </body>
  </html>
</xsl:template>

These templates use XPath expressions in their match attributes to specify which elements they apply to.

title: Value-of & For-Each
content:
XSLT Loops and Values

Extracting Data

The <xsl:value-of> element extracts the value of an XML element and adds it to the output. The <xsl:for-each> element lets you loop over matched elements to generate output for each one in sequence.

Looping Through Nodes
<xsl:for-each select="bookstore/book">
  <tr>
    <td><xsl:value-of select="title"/></td>
    <td><xsl:value-of select="price"/></td>
  </tr>
</xsl:for-each>

These constructs help iterate through data and insert values dynamically in the transformed result (like building table rows from a database!).

title: Conditional Logic
content:
XSLT Conditional Logic

Branching Output

To apply multiple conditions in XSLT transformations, you use the <xsl:choose> construct along with <xsl:when> and <xsl:otherwise>. This lets you perform branching logic based on XPath expressions evaluated during the transformation.

Choose Construct
<xsl:choose>
  <xsl:when test="price > 10">
    <td bgcolor="#ff00ff">
      <xsl:value-of select="title"/>
    </td>
  </xsl:when>
  <xsl:otherwise>
    <td><xsl:value-of select="title"/></td>
  </xsl:otherwise>
</xsl:choose>

Using these elements, you can output different results depending on conditions in the source XML.

title: XQuery Intro
content:
XML XQuery Introduction

Querying the Tree

XQuery (XML Query) is a language used to find and extract elements and attributes from XML documents — similar to how SQL queries data in a database. It is built directly on top of XPath expressions and allows complex data retrieval from XML structures.



XQuery is supported by many XML databases and tools for processing XML data.

title: XQuery Syntax
content:
XQuery Basic Syntax

The FLWOR Expression

XQuery syntax uses variables (prefixed with the $ sign) and a structure based on XPath. It is strictly case-sensitive, and expressions must follow XML naming rules for elements and variables.



The for, let, where, order by, and return clauses form a common XQuery pattern (known as FLWOR) for filtering and returning XML data.

title: XQuery Examples
content:
XQuery Example Usage

Extracting Data

An XQuery expression can select specific parts of an XML document using path expressions.

Filtering Books
for $x in doc("books.xml")/bookstore/book
where $x/price > 30
order by $x/title
return $x/title;

This expression extracts book titles from an XML file where the price is over 30, and returns them in sorted alphabetical order.

title: XQuery Functions
content:
XQuery Functions

Reusable Logic

XQuery supports built-in functions and user-defined functions to process XML data. Function calls can appear within expressions to manipulate strings, numbers, or nodes.

Declaring Functions
declare function local:minPrice($p as xs:decimal?)
  as xs:decimal?
{
  let $disc := ($p * 0.2)
  return ($p - $disc)
};

You can define your own functions using the declare function syntax for reusable logic and better structure in massive queries.

title: Final Summary
content:
XML Final Summary 🚀

Curriculum Complete! 🏆

This concludes your XML tutorial. XML (eXtensible Markup Language) is designed to store and transport data in a platform- and language-independent format. XML documents use a simple tree structure with elements, attributes, and nested content. Well-formed XML must follow strict syntax rules such as having a single root element, properly closed tags, and case-sensitive matching.



You learned how to build basic XML structures and how to use XML namespaces to avoid element naming conflicts. You also explored how to parse XML in browsers using the DOM model and how to load XML dynamically using XMLHttpRequest. The Document Object Model (DOM) lets you access, modify, add, and remove elements programmatically.

For querying and transforming XML, you learned XPath, which lets you select nodes and values in the XML tree, and XSLT, which allows transforming XML into other formats such as HTML. XQuery adds powerful querying capabilities similar to database SQL. For validation, both DTD and XML Schema (XSD) define rules and data types that an XML document must follow. XML is widely used for configuration files, data exchange, web services standards like SOAP and RSS, and many enterprise use cases where structured data is essential.

With these skills, you are now ready to create, manipulate, query, transform, and validate XML documents for real-world applications!
